title: Dynamic item hours distribution
excerpt:
It would be incredibly helpful to have enhanced control over how items are distributed within the workload widget view. 
For instance, if a team member has an 8-hour daily capacity and multiple tasks assigned throughout …

posts.author: rebekah.sykes (Rebekah)
posts.cookedHtml:
It would be incredibly helpful to have enhanced control over how items are distributed within the workload widget view.

For instance, if a team member has an 8-hour daily capacity and multiple tasks assigned throughout the week, I’d like the option to prioritise how the hours are allocated. For example, if one task requires 7 hours and another requires 4 hours, I’d like to prioritise the first task so that 6 hours are assigned to it on day 1, with the remaining 1 hour allocated to the second task. The remaining 3 hours of the second task would then carry over to day 2.

Currently, the system distributes task hours evenly across days, which can result in unbalanced workloads. In this scenario, day 1 ends up with 9 hours of work, while day 2 has only 2 hours, making it challenging to effectively manage resources.

posts.author: deepsangar (Deep Sangar)
posts.cookedHtml:
I completely agree with the frustration here, and I’m so glad it’s not just me! It’s also very annoying for the Creative Services team. This feels like it should be a really fundamental capability of a resource management system, so it’s quite baffling that we can’t control or prioritise how hours are distributed in a more practical way.

The current approach of evenly spreading time doesn’t reflect how work actually gets delivered day to day, and as you’ve outlined, it can quickly lead to unbalanced and unrealistic workloads. Being able to prioritise tasks and allocate time sequentially would make a huge difference in managing capacity more effectively.

I really urge Monday.com to look into this. I’ve had conversations with them and was told, “To clarify, the behavior you are experiencing is not a technical issue, so there is no error to resolve.” — which is honestly quite surprising.
